RQ1 
The introduction of semantically valid, artificially crafted, and maliciously altered network flow perturbations, creating the effects of a network poisoning attack, into an IDS training dataset, such as the Unified Multimodal NIDS CIC-IDS2019 dataset, reduces the robustness of the training of the model.

Train identical models using the same architecture and hyperparameters.

Group	Poisoning Level
Control	0% poisoned samples
Experiment 1	1% poisoned samples
Experiment 2	5% poisoned samples
Experiment 3	10% poisoned samples
Experiment 4	15% poisoned samples
Experiment 5	20% poisoned samples

Use:

Clean training set → baseline model
Poisoned training sets → experimental models
One identical clean test set for all evaluations

This isolates the effect of poisoning on learning.

Collect:

Accuracy
Precision
Recall
F1-score (macro and weighted)
ROC-AUC (OvR)
Confusion Matrix

Run each condition multiple times (e.g., 10–30 random seeds).

Calculate:

Mean
Standard deviation
95% confidence intervals

This demonstrates reproducibility.

To claim proof in a research context, show statistical significance.

Possible tests:

Paired t-test
Repeated Measures ANOVA
One-way ANOVA
Kruskal-Wallis (if non-normal)

Report:

p-values
Effect sizes (Cohen's d, η²)

Visualizations

Strong evidence often comes from plots:

Poisoning percentage vs. F1-score
Poisoning percentage vs. ROC-AUC
Poisoning percentage vs. attack success rate
Boxplots of repeated runs
Confidence interval plots

These clearly illustrate performance degradation trends.

The most convincing proof combines:

Controlled baseline (0% poison)
Increasing poisoning levels
Repeated runs
Statistical significance
Effect size reporting
Robustness testing on adversarial samples
Evidence that perturbations remain semantically valid

Models trained on datasets containing semantically valid adversarial perturbations exhibited statistically significant reductions in accuracy, macro F1 score, and adversarial robustness as poisoning levels increased. The observed degradation followed a dose-dependent pattern, providing empirical evidence that poisoning attacks compromise IDS model robustness.
